title: WLC 115-117: The Fourth Commandment — The Christian Sabbath
speaker: Mr. Isaiah Couch
source_files: 08-06-23 - Adult Sunday School - WLC 115, 116, 117 - Mr. Isaiah Couch.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Commandments Are Not Burdensome (WLC Q115)
subpoints: 1 John 5:1-4: His commandments are not burdensome
Psalm 119:165: Great peace have those who love your law
Matthew 11:28-30: My yoke is easy and my burden is light
WLC Q115: The fourth commandment — Remember the Sabbath day to keep it holy (Exodus 20:8-11)

outline.title: Is the Sabbath Commandment Perpetual or Temporary? (WLC Q116)
subpoints: The catechism requires all men to sanctify one whole day in seven — the Christian Sabbath, now the Lord's Day
Reason 1: The Sabbath's foundation is creation, not Sinai (Genesis 2:1-3; Exodus 20:11) — this grounds it as perpetual for all creation
Sabbath observed before Sinai: in Genesis 2 at creation, and in Exodus 16 before the law was given
Reason 2: The fourth commandment is part of the Ten Commandments (moral law) — it cannot be ceremonial while nine are moral
The Ten Commandments form a unity; the Sabbath was written on stone, indicating permanence
Reason 3: Isaiah 56 speaks of Sabbath observance in the New Testament era, indicating it is not temporary
The change from the seventh day to the first day: effected by Christ's resurrection, not Constantine or human tradition
J.J. Voss: Christ buried the Old Testament seventh-day Sabbath in the tomb and rose with the New Testament Sabbath on the first day
Evidence for the Lord's Day: Revelation 1:10, Luke 24, John 20, Paul's instructions in 1 Corinthians 16 and Acts 20
Psalm 118:22-24 applied to the resurrection: "This is the day the LORD has made" — the cornerstone laid on the first day

outline.title: How Is the Sabbath to Be Sanctified? (WLC Q117)
subpoints: Negative observance: holy resting from sinful works, worldly employment, and lawful recreations
The Sabbath was made for man (Mark 2:27) — resting from worldly things is for our good
"A holy resting" does not mean idleness but spiritual activity in public and private worship
The Puritans: the Sabbath is a "market day for the soul"
Isaiah 58:13-14: call the Sabbath a delight, honor it — then you shall delight yourself in the Lord
Positive observance: spending the whole day in public and private exercises of God's worship
Works of necessity and mercy are permitted (Luke 14: pulling an ox from a pit)
J.J. Voss on necessity: tasks that cannot be postponed (milking cows, putting out fires, repairing a car to avoid greater labor on the Sabbath)
Works of mercy: caring for the sick, with compassion as the motive rather than financial gain
Preparation for the Sabbath: prayer during the week, reading appointed scriptures, going to bed early Saturday, settling tithes and offerings beforehand

summary:
In this first of a two-part lesson on the fourth commandment, Mr. Isaiah Couch uses Westminster Larger Catechism questions 115 through 117 to examine what the Sabbath commandment is, why it is perpetual and binding on all creation, and how it is to be sanctified. He opens by establishing that the commandments — including the fourth — are not burdensome, citing 1 John 5:1-4, Psalm 119:165, and Matthew 11:28-30, and honestly sharing his own initial resistance to the Reformed understanding of Sabbath observance.

The substantial middle section addresses the objection that the Sabbath is a temporary ceremonial law given only to Israel. Couch offers three lines of evidence for its perpetual, moral character: first, the Sabbath's foundation in creation (Genesis 2:1-3) predates Sinai, and the Sabbath was already being observed in Exodus 16 before the giving of the law; second, as one of the Ten Commandments written on stone, it cannot be ceremonial when the other nine are clearly moral; third, Isaiah 56 prophetically anticipates Sabbath observance in the New Testament era. He then addresses the change from the seventh day to the first, arguing from J.J. Voss that Christ himself, through his resurrection, buried the Old Testament Sabbath and inaugurated the Lord's Day on the first day of the week — evidenced by apostolic practice in Revelation 1:10, Luke 24, John 20, and Paul's instructions.

The lesson concludes with WLC Q117 on how to sanctify the Lord's Day, covering both negative observance (resting from worldly employment and recreation) and positive observance (spending the day in public and private worship). Practical application includes preparing for the Sabbath on Saturday, the permissibility of works of necessity and mercy, and the Puritan concept of the Sabbath as "a market day for the soul." Questions 115 through 117 are covered; question 118 is reserved for the next lesson.
transcript:
## Opening

Good morning. Happy Lord's Day. I want to thank the session and everyone involved in asking me to speak during the Sunday school hour, to lead a couple of lessons during the moral law series. I always enjoy being asked to do that.

## Introduction: His Commandments Are Not Burdensome

Before we begin, I want to read 1 John 5:1-4: "Whoever believes that Jesus is the Christ is born of God, and everyone who loves him who begot God also loves him who is begotten of God. By this we know that we love the children of God, when we love God and keep his commandments. For this is the love of God, that we keep his commandments, and his commandments are not burdensome. For whatever is born of God overcomes the world, and this is the victory that has overcome the world — our faith."

I hope we understand that this is the love of God, that we keep his commandments, and that his commandments are not burdensome in any way. When Pastor T2 preached through Psalm 119, one of my favorite verses he addressed was verse 165: "Great peace have those who love your law, and nothing causes them to stumble." When we read the law of God and meditate upon it, we should rejoice — not because we have personally kept it perfectly, but because we have a perfect Savior who kept it perfectly and has clothed us in his righteousness. Jesus said, "Come to me all you who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart, and you will find rest for your souls, for my yoke is easy and my burden is light."

As I said, this morning begins a two-part section on the fourth commandment addressing the Sabbath and the duties required in it. I hope it does not come across as a burden, but that we would rejoice in this great treasure — made for our good and for his glory.

## WLC Q115: Which Is the Fourth Commandment?

Question 115: "Which is the fourth commandment?" Answer: "The fourth commandment is: Remember the Sabbath day, to keep it holy. Six days you shall labor and do all your work, but the seventh day is the Sabbath of the LORD your God. In it you shall do no work: you, nor your son, nor your daughter, nor your male servant, nor your female servant, nor your cattle, nor your stranger who is within your gates. For in six days the LORD made the heavens and the earth, the sea, and all that is in them, and rested on the seventh day. Therefore the LORD blessed the seventh day and hallowed it."

The fourth commandment is found in Exodus 20:8-11 and in a parallel passage in Deuteronomy 5. The two differences between these passages are at the end of the commandment: Exodus 20 focuses on God as Creator ("in six days the LORD made the heavens and the earth"), while Deuteronomy 5 focuses on God as Redeemer ("remember that you were a slave in Egypt... therefore the LORD your God commanded you to keep the Sabbath day"). In his Sunday school series through the Christian Sabbath, Pastor T2 did a great job of explaining how the Sabbath is observed because God is both Creator and Redeemer — we will unpack that more fully in the final question next week.

## WLC Q116: What Is Required in the Fourth Commandment?

Question 116: "What is required in the fourth commandment?" Answer: "The fourth commandment requires of all men the sanctifying or keeping holy to God such set times as he has appointed in his word; expressly one whole day in seven, which was the seventh from the beginning of the world to the resurrection of Christ, and the first day of the week ever since, and so to continue to the end of the world — which is the Christian Sabbath, and in the New Testament called the Lord's Day."

Wilhelmus à Brakel, the 17th-century Dutch theologian, once wrote: "This is the commandment which is contradicted by many and is assaulted with highly elevated shields. If many would have had it in their power, it would have long been forgotten and rejected as is true for all the other commandments." I must be completely honest: when I first really began to study the Reformed understanding of the Sabbath day, I raised my shield higher than everyone else. "How dare you tell me what I can and cannot do on the Sabbath? This is my day off." After reading the Westminster Confession of Faith, the Larger Catechism, à Brakel's four-volume series, and Thomas Watson's commentary on the commandments, I really struggled with it. Many of my objections were valid; but many were sinful and selfish.

The key question: is the fourth commandment perpetual (moral law) or temporal (ceremonial)? The answer can be demonstrated three ways.

**First, the fourth commandment's foundation is creation, not Sinai.** Exodus 20:11 summarizes Genesis 2:1-3: "Thus the heavens and the earth and all the host of them were finished. And on the seventh day God ended his work which he had done, and he rested on the seventh day from all his work which he had done. Then God blessed the seventh day and sanctified it." The Sabbath was established at creation and is binding on all creation — not just on Israel from Sinai. Was it observed before Sinai? Yes. In Genesis 2, Adam and all creation witnessed God blessing and sanctifying the seventh day. Mark 2:27 says, "The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath." The Sabbath was created on day seven for the benefit of man, who was created on day six. There is also Exodus 16, four chapters before the law at Sinai: Israel has left Egypt, is in the wilderness, and God provides manna — giving double portions on the sixth day and commanding rest on the seventh. When some went out on the Sabbath to gather, God said, "How long do you refuse to keep my commandments?" The Sabbath was already in effect.

**Second, as part of the Ten Commandments, the Sabbath must be regarded as moral law.** To present nine of the ten as perpetual and one as temporary is inconsistent. We can demonstrate this with three examples. We would never say that murder was permissible before Sinai because the sixth commandment wasn't given yet — the unjust taking of life is grounded in the creation of man in God's image (Genesis 9:6), and God condemned Cain's murder of Abel (Genesis 4:9). We would never say adultery was permissible before Sinai — it is grounded in Genesis 2:24, the one-flesh union. The first commandment itself is grounded in creation: Genesis 2:16-17, where God commands Adam, and Adam's sin in following the serpent's word rather than God's. J.J. Voss: "The ten commandments form a unity as a summary of God's moral law. If the Sabbath commandment were not moral law, this unity would be broken." Furthermore, the commandments were written on stone by God himself — indicating their permanent validity. All parts of the ceremonial law passed away at the crucifixion; but these commandments were written on stone, showing they were intended to be permanent.

**Third, Scripture speaks prophetically of Sabbath observance in the New Testament.** Isaiah 56, speaking of the New Testament era, says: "Blessed is the man who does this... who keeps from defiling the Sabbath, and keeps his hand from doing evil." This passage describes Jew and Gentile, slave and free, rich and poor all laying hold of Christ's salvation — which is our era — and calling them to keep the Sabbath.

**A common question: Who made the change from the seventh day to the first?** Some say no explicit command mandates the change and therefore the Sabbath and Lord's Day cannot be connected. Some say Constantine made the change in 321 AD. Others say the apostles chose to meet on the first day with no connection to the Old Testament Sabbath. Voss gives the best answer: it was our Lord Jesus Christ, by the accomplishment of his great redemptive work, who brought about the change. Christ buried the Old Testament seventh-day Sabbath in the tomb with himself and left it there; when he arose, he brought with him the New Testament Sabbath to be observed on the first day. We see this in the New Testament:

- Paul expected the churches in Galatia and Corinth to gather and collect offerings on the first day of the week.
- John in Revelation 1:10 says he was "in the Spirit on the Lord's Day."
- Luke 24 and John 20: the disciples were gathered together on the first day — the day of the resurrection — and Jesus appeared to them. It almost seems as if Jesus returned the following first day specifically to include Thomas.
- Psalm 118:22-24: "The stone which the builders rejected has become the chief cornerstone. This was the LORD's doing; it is marvelous in our eyes. This is the day the LORD has made; we will rejoice and be glad in it." The New Testament repeatedly applies this passage to Christ's resurrection. The day the LORD made — the day of the living cornerstone — is the first day.

I also recommend *The Sanctification of the Sabbath* by Robert Halding, published by Reformation Heritage, which demonstrates thoroughly how the change from the seventh to the first day does not invalidate the commandment but actually fulfills it.

## WLC Q117: How Is the Sabbath or Lord's Day to Be Sanctified?

Question 117: "How is the Sabbath or the Lord's Day to be sanctified?" Answer: "The Sabbath or Lord's Day is to be sanctified by a holy resting all the day, not only from such works as are at all times sinful, but even from such worldly employment and recreations as are on other days lawful; and making it our delight to spend the whole time, except so much of it as is to be taken up in works of necessity and mercy, in the public and private exercises of God's worship; and to that end, we are to prepare our hearts, and with such foresight, diligence, and moderation to dispose and seasonably dispatch our worldly business, that we may be the more free and fit for the duties of that day."

The catechism speaks of both negative and positive observance of the Lord's Day.

**Negative observance**: holy resting from sinful works, worldly employment, and lawful recreations. "A holy resting" does not mean idleness; it means being physically at rest from worldly labor so as to be spiritually active in obedience to God. The Puritans called the Sabbath a "market day for the soul" — the day believers gather in the marketplace of God and are refreshed, nourished, and strengthened with spiritual food for the week. Isaiah 58:13-14: "If you turn away your foot from the Sabbath, from doing your pleasure on my holy day, and call the Sabbath a delight, the holy day of the LORD, honorable... then you shall delight yourself in the LORD."

I must be honest: when I first encountered this, I pushed back hard. But ceasing from worldly employment and recreation is for our good. The Sabbath was made for man. And as Andrew pointed out a couple of months ago when he injured his foot playing sports on the Lord's Day — well, I'll talk to him privately about that.

**Works of necessity and mercy**: The catechism permits works of necessity and mercy. Voss: if a house catches fire, it must be extinguished. Cows must be milked. Even work that might otherwise be postponed may be done on the Sabbath if it prevents greater work on the Sabbath — for example, spending fifteen minutes repairing a car to avoid two hours of alternative transportation to church. Works of mercy are done primarily from compassion rather than financial gain: physicians and nurses may care for the sick; visitors may call on the sick. Jesus in Luke 14 asked the Pharisees: "Which of you, having a donkey or an ox that has fallen into a pit, will not immediately pull him out on the Sabbath?" We cannot, however, fit everything we want into "works of necessity and mercy." The focus of the day is worship.

**Positive observance**: Making it our delight to spend the whole time in public and private exercises of God's worship. Psalm 122:1: "I was glad when they said to me, 'Let us go into the house of the LORD.'" To prepare our hearts and dispatch our worldly business before the Sabbath — so we may be free and fit for its duties. Practical suggestions:

- Pray during the week for the Lord's Day duties.
- Read the appointed scriptures beforehand and meditate on them.
- Speak with your children about the gift of the Lord's Day.
- Run errands on Friday or Saturday; fill the car with fuel before Sunday; set aside tithes and offerings beforehand.
- Go to bed early Saturday so body and mind are renewed.

Just as we prepare for a vacation, a birthday party, or a wedding anniversary — how much more should we prepare for the market day of the soul?

I am going to save question 118 for next week. Are there any questions about the three questions we've covered today?

*Closing prayer.*
